item.title: CVE-2026-33825 ransomware exploitation, Kemp LoadMaster RCE, and Glitch SPY Android RAT
item.content:encoded:
CISA: Windows BlueHammer flaw now exploited by ransomware gangs — Must Read

CISA says ransomware gangs are now exploiting BlueHammer, a Microsoft Defender local privilege escalation flaw that can give attackers SYSTEM-level control of Windows machines. Microsoft patched CVE-2026-33825 in April, but organizations should ensure affected systems are updated immediately.

  • CISA confirmed that ransomware gangs have begun exploiting the high-severity Microsoft Defender privilege escalation vulnerability known as BlueHammer, tracked as CVE-2026-33825.
  • The BlueHammer flaw was leaked in early April by a security researcher known as Nightmare Eclipse along with proof-of-concept exploit code.
  • Microsoft patched CVE-2026-33825 on April 14 as part of the April 2026 Patch Tuesday updates.
  • CISA added BlueHammer to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog on April 22 and ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch affected Windows devices by May 7.
  • CISA has now updated its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to flag CVE-2026-33825 as exploited in ransomware campaigns, while Microsoft has not yet tagged the flaw as exploited in attacks.

Progress Kemp LoadMaster Flaw Could Let Attackers Run Root Commands Pre-Auth — Must Read

Progress Kemp LoadMaster has a critical pre-auth root command execution flaw, CVE-2026-8037, affecting systems with the API enabled. Admins should upgrade to GA v7.2.63.2 or LTSF v7.2.54.18 immediately, especially now that a public proof of concept exists.

  • CVE-2026-8037 is a critical Progress Kemp LoadMaster vulnerability with a CVSS score of 9.8 that can allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary commands as root through a crafted API request.
  • The flaw affects LoadMaster GA v7.2.63.1 and older and LTSF v7.2.54.17 and older when the API is enabled, and Progress fixed it in GA v7.2.63.2 and LTSF v7.2.54.18.
  • The vulnerability is in the escape_quotes() function, which allocated an uncleared memory buffer and failed to add a null terminator after sanitized input.
  • Progress said it had not received reports of exploitation, but watchTowr Labs published a detailed technical breakdown with a working proof of concept on June 29.
  • Progress also patched CVE-2026-33691, a high-severity WAF bypass involving whitespace padding in filenames that could circumvent file upload extension checks.

Apple Patches 30+ iOS, macOS, Safari Flaws, Including AI-Discovered WebKit Bugs — Should Read

Apple released early security updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS Tahoe, and Safari to fix more than 30 flaws, including AI-discovered WebKit bugs that could crash Safari or corrupt memory via malicious web content. Apple said it is accelerating patches because AI may shrink the time between vulnerability disclosure and exploit weaponization.

  • Apple released security updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS Tahoe, and Safari to address over three dozen flaws.
  • Four WebKit vulnerabilities discovered using AI tools such as Anthropic Claude and OpenAI Codex Security were included in the fixes.
  • The AI-discovered WebKit flaws include memory corruption, Safari crash, out-of-bounds write, and use-after-free issues triggered by maliciously crafted web content.
  • Apple also fixed kernel-related bugs that could let a malicious app leak sensitive kernel state, cause unexpected system termination, write kernel memory, or corrupt kernel memory.
  • Apple said none of the patched vulnerabilities has been disclosed as actively exploited in the wild.

Blackfield ransomware asks Nidec Corporation for $2 million ransom — Should Read

Blackfield ransomware claims it breached Nidec’s Taiwanese subsidiary and is demanding $2 million to delete allegedly stolen data, with a threat to leak or sell it. Nidec confirmed ransomware damage to part of the subsidiary’s server and is investigating possible data leakage and operational impact.

  • The Blackfield ransomware gang is demanding a $2 million ransom from Nidec Corporation after claiming an attack on its Taiwanese subsidiary, Nidec Chaun Choung Technology.
  • Nidec said ransomware-originated damage was confirmed on part of Nidec Chaun Choung Technology’s server on June 22, 2026, and emergency measures included shutting down the affected server and network.
  • Nidec disclosed a possibility of information leakage but said no personal or confidential information has been confirmed as leaked online.
  • Blackfield threatened to publish or sell the allegedly stolen data if Nidec does not respond, offered a one-day deadline extension for $5,000, and listed immediate data access for $400,000.
  • Nidec is investigating possible impacts on production, shipping, and other operations but does not expect the incident to affect other Nidec Corporation or Nidec Group companies.

New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials — Should Read

LayerX researchers found that a prompt-injection technique called BioShocking can trick AI browsers in agent mode into copying sensitive data from signed-in accounts, such as GitHub credentials, and sending it to an attacker. The key risk is that AI agents often cannot reliably distinguish user instructions from malicious webpage content, so their access should be tightly limited and confirmed before touching private data.

  • Security firm LayerX disclosed BioShocking, a technique that tricked six AI browsers and assistants into copying a user’s credentials and sending them to an attacker.
  • The targets tested included OpenAI’s ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity’s Comet, and Anthropic’s Claude browser extension.
  • The attack used a puzzle-themed malicious web page that rewarded wrong answers and ultimately instructed the agent to grab the user’s credentials.
  • In LayerX’s test, the agent followed a link to the victim’s work GitHub repository, pulled SSH login credentials from a harmless plaintext file, and passed them to the attacker.
  • LayerX said OpenAI fixed the issue in ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity closed the report without action, Fellou, Genspark, and Sigma did not respond, and Anthropic’s attempted fix for the Claude extension did not hold.

Glitch SPY: An Emerging Android RAT Distributed Through a Fake Polish Rental App — Should Read

Cyble identified Glitch SPY, an emerging Android RAT spread through a fake Polish rental app and delivered via the Brokewell loader. Once installed, it abuses Android Accessibility Service to gain extensive remote-control and spying capabilities, including screen streaming, SMS and credential theft, crypto-clipper activity, file access, and hidden browser-based account takeover from the victim’s own device.

  • Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs identified an emerging Android malware family called Glitch SPY that was distributed through a fake Polish apartment and house rental website at tutaj-dompl[.]com.
  • The downloaded application was identified as the Brokewell Android Loader, which acts as a dropper and installs the Glitch SPY payload after persuading the user to allow installation from unknown sources.
  • Glitch SPY abuses Android Accessibility Service to automate permission grants, interact with the device interface, extract visible screen content, perform gestures, and support remote input.
  • Glitch SPY maintains a persistent WebSocket connection to its command-and-control server and supports more than 70 commands, including screen streaming, SMS theft, contact and call log collection, location tracking, camera and microphone access, keylogging, file management, and shell execution.
  • The malware includes a crypto-clipper that replaces copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses and a hidden remote-browser feature that can let attackers conduct web-based account takeover from the victim’s own device and IP address.

Kali Linux 2026.2 released with 9 new tools, NetHunter updates — May Read

Kali Linux 2026.2 is now available with 9 new security tools, a Linux 6.19 kernel, updated GNOME and KDE desktops, faster VM boot times, and expanded Kali NetHunter support. The release mainly improves penetration testing workflows across desktops, virtual machines, and Android devices.

  • Kali Linux 2026.2 is now available for download as the second release of the year, with 9 new tools and numerous Kali NetHunter improvements.
  • The release upgrades the Kali kernel to 6.19, with the 7.0 kernel available in kali-experimental, and adds tools including arsenal-ng, hydra-gtk, legba, oletools, penelope, shell-gpt, tailscale, tookie-osint, and uro.
  • Kali Linux 2026.2 updates service helper scripts to make it easier to manage services, check running status, track service state, list default credentials, and get access information for running services.
  • Pre-built Kali Linux VM images no longer include graphics firmware, reducing the initrd to 60 MB for VM users and cutting boot time by about three times in the Kali Team’s QEMU VM test on a Linux host.
  • Kali NetHunter updates include Magisk standalone kernel flashing support, new kernels with qcacld3 injection support, support for more phones and versions, NetHunter Pro bare metal support on more phones, and a new EvilTwin tab with a password verification captive portal.

Silent Push partners with Vexelon to bring preemptive cyber defense to the Western Balkans — Optional

Silent Push has partnered with Vexelon to integrate its preemptive cyber defense platform into Vexelon’s managed SOC services across the Western Balkans. The partnership aims to help organizations detect adversary infrastructure before attacks reach their networks, especially in a region facing persistent geopolitical cyber threats.

  • Silent Push announced a strategic partnership with Vexelon to bring the Silent Push Preemptive Cyber Defense platform into Vexelon’s managed SOC operations in the Western Balkans.
  • Vexelon provides round-the-clock security operations for organizations across North Macedonia and Kosovo, including SOC-as-a-Service, managed detection and response, attack surface management, incident response, and compliance readiness.
  • The article says the Western Balkans has faced targeted cyber threats tied to geopolitical tensions, including incidents affecting Montenegro, Albania, Kosovo, and North Macedonia.
  • Silent Push says its Context Graph maps DNS, WHOIS, certificates, and hosting data to identify Indicators of Future Attack before adversary infrastructure is used against a client.
  • Silent Push states that Vexelon’s SOC now has an average of 154 days of lead time before a campaign reaches a client’s perimeter.

item.title: Oracle E-Business Suite CVE-2026-46817 Actively Exploited After Patch Release
ShinyHunters PeopleSoft breaches, Kemp LoadMaster CVE-2026-8037, and LLM security tooling
U.S. Targets Russian Signal/WhatsApp Hackers as WhatsApp Adds Usernames, Mustang Panda Hits India
U.S. Bounties Russian Messaging Hackers as Mustang Panda and SimpleHelp Exploits Surface
Microsoft Purges StegoAd Edge Extensions as libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 PoC Emerges
